output.council_question:
We need a self-contained proof of the finite weighted Bernoulli inequality: for \(0\le p\le 1/3\), nonnegative \(w_i\) summing to \(1\), and independent \(v_i\sim\mathrm{Bernoulli}(p)\),
\[
\Pr\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p.
\]
Equivalently, if \(L=1/p\ge3\), \(0\le a_i\le1\), \(\sum_i a_i=L\), and \(\xi_i\sim\mathrm{Bernoulli}(1/L)\), prove
\[
\Pr\left[\sum_i a_i\xi_i\ge1\right]\ge 1/L.
\]
The reciprocal/integer \(L\) case has a coloring proof. Please suggest viable strategies for the non-integral \(L>3\) case, ideally a complete proof or a precise known theorem. Be wary of two false deterministic strengthenings: (1) rational color weights do not satisfy the desired fixed-size subset bound in general; e.g. \(a=3,b=10\), seven entries \(1/7\), three \(0\); (2) a deterministic circle-arc start-measure analogue is false.

output.text:
I would pivot to the following finite “Kellerer/fractional Hall” formulation. It is exactly tailored to the non-integral case and avoids the two false deterministic strengthenings.

**Finite Kellerer lemma.** Let \(0<p\le 1/3\) and let \(\mathcal D\subseteq 2^{[m]}\) be a down-set. If
\[
\mu_p(\mathcal D)>1-p
\]
for the product \(p\)-measure, then there is a probability distribution \(\nu\) supported on \(\mathcal D\) such that every coordinate has marginal \(p\):
\[
\nu\{A\in\mathcal D:i\in A\}=p\qquad(i=1,\dots,m).
\]
Equivalently, \((p,\ldots,p)\in \operatorname{conv}\{\mathbf 1_A:A\in\mathcal D\}\).

Given this lemma, your desired inequality is immediate. In the normalized form, put \(p=1/L\) and
\[
\mathcal D=\left\{A\subseteq[m]:\sum_{i\in A}a_i<1\right\}.
\]
This is a down-set. If the Bernoulli inequality failed, then
\[
\mu_p(\mathcal D)=\Pr\!\left[\sum_i a_i\xi_i<1\right]>1-p.
\]
Kellerer gives \(\nu\) on \(\mathcal D\) with all marginals \(p\). Hence
\[
1=p\sum_i a_i
  =\sum_i a_i\,\nu(i\in A)
  =\mathbb E_\nu\sum_{i\in A}a_i
  <1,
\]
a contradiction. This proves all real \(L\ge3\), with no rational approximation or continuity issue.

This lemma is also sharp in exactly the right way: for \(p>1/3\), take \(\mathcal D=\{\varnothing,\{1\},\{2\},\{3\}\}\). Then \(\mu_p(\mathcal D)>1-p\) for \(p<1/2\), but \((p,p,p)\notin\operatorname{conv}(\mathcal D)\) because every vector in the convex hull has coordinate sum at most \(1\).

I am fairly sure this is the “analogous regions” theorem you want from Kellerer, but I would cite only this precise convex-hull/marginal statement, not the paper vaguely. If you need self-containedness, make this lemma the main lemma and reproduce Kellerer’s finite induction/LP proof. Note, however, that by separation this lemma is essentially dual to your weighted Bernoulli inequality, so a proof that merely invokes separating hyperplanes would be circular; the useful content is Kellerer’s \(p\le1/3\) convex-hull theorem itself.

output.council_question:
We need to prove the small-p lemma:

For \(0\le p\le1/3\), all nonnegative weights \(w_i\) summing to \(1\), and iid \(v_i\sim\mathrm{Bernoulli}(p)\),
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p.
\]

Equivalent normalized form: with \(L=1/p\ge3\), \(a_i=w_i/p\), \(\sum a_i=L\), and \(\xi_i\sim\mathrm{Bernoulli}(1/L)\), prove
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i a_i\xi_i\ge1\right]\ge 1/L.
\]
If any \(a_i\ge1\), this is immediate; hard case \(0\le a_i<1\). By multiplicative closure it suffices to prove \(p\in[1/6,1/3]\), i.e. \(L\in[3,6]\).

Complement form: with \(q=1-p\ge2/3\), prove
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i\eta_i>q\right]\le q,\qquad \eta_i\sim\mathrm{Bernoulli}(q).
\]
This is a weighted-threshold hypotenuse problem. For the bad down-set
\[
\mathcal D=\{A:\sum_{i\in A}w_i<p\},
\]
we know \(\mu_p(\mathcal D)\le1-p\) is desired; also for \(r=\lfloor1/p\rfloor\), no \(r\) members of \(\mathcal D\) cover \([m]\), so the complement threshold family is \(r\)-wise intersecting, but ordinary \(r\)-wise intersection alone is insufficient.

Please suggest a rigorous proof strategy. Especially valuable would be:
1. a self-contained proof for \(L\in[3,6]\);
2. a theorem from extremal set theory / biased measure of weighted threshold families that implies it;
3. a way to adapt Fokkink–Meester–Pelekis cyclic/injection arguments from special rational points to all \(q=t\ge2/3\);
4. or a sharp induction/pivot inequality proof:
\[
\Pr[Y\ge p]\ge \frac{p}{1-p}\Pr[Y<p-a].
\]

input.author_question:
We need to prove the small-p lemma:

For \(0\le p\le1/3\), all nonnegative weights \(w_i\) summing to \(1\), and iid \(v_i\sim\mathrm{Bernoulli}(p)\),
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p.
\]

Equivalent normalized form: with \(L=1/p\ge3\), \(a_i=w_i/p\), \(\sum a_i=L\), and \(\xi_i\sim\mathrm{Bernoulli}(1/L)\), prove
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i a_i\xi_i\ge1\right]\ge 1/L.
\]
If any \(a_i\ge1\), this is immediate; hard case \(0\le a_i<1\). By multiplicative closure it suffices to prove \(p\in[1/6,1/3]\), i.e. \(L\in[3,6]\).

Complement form: with \(q=1-p\ge2/3\), prove
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i\eta_i>q\right]\le q,\qquad \eta_i\sim\mathrm{Bernoulli}(q).
\]
This is a weighted-threshold hypotenuse problem. For the bad down-set
\[
\mathcal D=\{A:\sum_{i\in A}w_i<p\},
\]
we know \(\mu_p(\mathcal D)\le1-p\) is desired; also for \(r=\lfloor1/p\rfloor\), no \(r\) members of \(\mathcal D\) cover \([m]\), so the complement threshold family is \(r\)-wise intersecting, but ordinary \(r\)-wise intersection alone is insufficient.

Please suggest a rigorous proof strategy. Especially valuable would be:
1. a self-contained proof for \(L\in[3,6]\);
2. a theorem from extremal set theory / biased measure of weighted threshold families that implies it;
3. a way to adapt Fokkink–Meester–Pelekis cyclic/injection arguments from special rational points to all \(q=t\ge2/3\);
4. or a sharp induction/pivot inequality proof:
\[
\Pr[Y\ge p]\ge \frac{p}{1-p}\Pr[Y<p-a].
\]

We need prove or refute the remaining small-p lemma:

For \(0\le p\le 1/3\), \(w_i\ge0\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), and iid \(v_i\sim\mathrm{Bernoulli}(p)\), prove
\[
\Pr\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p.
\]

Equivalently, for \(q=1-p\ge2/3\), prove
\[
\Pr\left[\sum_i w_i\eta_i>q\right]\le q,\qquad \eta_i\sim\mathrm{Bernoulli}(q).
\]

A promising reduction added this round: define
\[
\Phi_q(w)=\Pr_q\left[\sum_i w_i\eta_i>q\right].
\]
If two weights \(x\le y\) are coalesced to \(x+y\), and \(Z\) is the contribution of the other weights, then
\[
\Phi_q(\ldots,x+y,\ldots)-\Phi_q(\ldots,x,y,\ldots)
=pq\left(\Pr[q-x-y<Z\le q-y]-\Pr[q-x<Z\le q]\right),
\]
up to endpoint conventions. If for every non-unit vector and every \(q\ge2/3\) some pair can be coalesced without decreasing \(\Phi_q\), repeated coalescence proves the lemma because the one-weight vector has \(\Phi_q=q\). Random searches support existence of such a pair, though arbitrary prescribed pairs can fail.

Please suggest a rigorous proof strategy for either the small-\(p\) lemma directly or this coalescence assertion. I am especially interested in: (1) an induction or averaging proof that some pair has nonnegative coalescence gain; (2) a known theorem about biased product measures of halfspaces through their mean; (3) a combinatorial injection/shadow argument for the strict complement inequality; or (4) a Bellman/dynamic-programming invariant stronger than the failed scalar minorant.

output.council_question:
The remaining core problem is the unit-gap/two-tail inequality equivalent to the small-\(p\) lemma: for \(0<p\le 1/3\), \(q=1-p\), \(0\le b_i\le1\), \(B=\sum b_i\), and \(\zeta_i\sim\mathrm{Bernoulli}(p)\), prove
\[
\Pr\!\left[\sum_i b_i(\zeta_i-p)\ge p\right]
\ge {p\over q}\Pr\!\left[\sum_i b_i(\zeta_i-p)<-q\right].
\]
This resembles a martingale/ballot inequality for jumps \(+qb_i\) with probability \(p\) and \(-pb_i\) with probability \(q\), with all jump ranges at most \(1\). A naive shifted induction
\[
\Pr[S\ge x+p]\ge(p/q)\Pr[S<x-q]
\]
closes under conditioning but its base case fails for shifts \(x>q\); a restricted domain closes only when the remaining total \(B\le q/p\), not in the hard dust regime. Can you suggest a rigorous proof route for this two-tail inequality, or a precise known theorem (Kellerer/Kanter/Pinelis/ballot/size-bias/etc.) that implies it? If you think the inequality is false, give a concrete counterexample; otherwise prioritize proof strategies that could fit in a short LaTeX solution.

\mathbb E\,\#\{j:L_j<r/b\}\le b-r.

The corresponding deterministic pointwise interval count is false.

output.council_question:
We need close the remaining theorem for the weighted Bernoulli problem. A useful equivalent form is:

For \(0<p\le 1/3\), \(q=1-p\), and any finite coefficients \(0\le b_i\le 1\), with \(\zeta_i\) iid Bernoulli\((p)\), prove
\[
q\,\Pr\!\left[\sum_i b_i(\zeta_i-p)\ge p\right]
\ge
p\,\Pr\!\left[\sum_i b_i(\zeta_i-p)<-q\right].
\tag{CTC}
\]
This is equivalent to the missing positive direction. A naive pointwise subharmonic proof for \(g(y)=q1_{y\ge p}-p1_{y<-q}\) fails near \(-q\); the shifted induction only proves a subcritical range. Please suggest a portfolio of rigorous approaches to prove CTC (or an equivalent theorem): martingale/Bellman potentials, extremal halfspace/threshold-family arguments, known inequalities for sums of bounded Bernoulli variables, or combinatorial convex-hull/partition-function methods. Focus on ideas that could plausibly yield a short self-contained proof.

output.council_question:
We now have a proved reduction: for \(\lambda=p/(1-p)\le 1/2\), it suffices to prove the partition-function bound
\[
p{\bf1}\notin P(\mathcal D)\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
Z_\lambda(\mathcal D)\le (1+\lambda)^{n-1}
\]
for shifted down-sets \(\mathcal D\subseteq2^{[n]}\), where \(p=\lambda/(1+\lambda)\) and \(P(\mathcal D)=\operatorname{conv}\{1_A:A\in\mathcal D\}\).

For a shifted slice decomposition
\[
\mathcal D=\mathcal E\cup\{A\cup\{n+1\}:A\in\mathcal F\},\qquad
\mathcal F\subseteq\mathcal E,
\]
shiftedness also gives that \(\mathcal E\) contains the upper shadow of \(\mathcal F\). The induction reduces to the mixed case
\[
p{\bf1}\in P(\mathcal E),\qquad p{\bf1}\notin P(\mathcal F),\qquad
\mu_p(\mathcal E)+\lambda\mu_p(\mathcal F)>1.
\]
Can you suggest a proof of this shifted theorem, especially a way to close the mixed slice case? Useful angles might include shifted-complex polytopes, fractional matching/covering duality, shadow inequalities, or a strengthened capped-slice lemma.

   \[
   p{\bf1}\notin P(\mathcal D),\ \mathcal D\text{ shifted down-set},\ \lambda=p/(1-p)\le1/2
   \Rightarrow Z_\lambda(\mathcal D)\le(1+\lambda)^{n-1}.
   \]
   Keywords: shifted simplicial complexes, independence polytopes of shifted complexes, fractional matching number, fractional covering, p-biased measure, Erdős matching conjecture, Frankl shifted families, weighted EKR, threshold complexes.
3. Computationally enumerate shifted down-sets as far as feasible (at least through \(n=6\), ideally \(n=7\) if optimized) and, for a grid of \(p\in\{1/6,1/5,1/4,0.3,1/3\}\), solve LP membership for \(p{\bf1}\in P(\mathcal D)\). Record the extremal non-convex shifted families by \(\mu_p\), their facets, separating weights, and whether the maximum is always the dictatorship.
4. Analyze the shifted slice induction:
   \[
   \mathcal D=\mathcal E\cup(\mathcal F+(n+1)),\quad \mathcal E\supseteq \partial^+\mathcal F.
   \]
   Try to prove or disprove the mixed-slice implication
   \[
   p{\bf1}\in P(\mathcal E),\ p{\bf1}\notin P(\mathcal F),\
   \mu_p(\mathcal E)+\lambda\mu_p(\mathcal F)>1
   \Rightarrow p{\bf1}\in P(\mathcal D).
   \]
   If false, find the smallest counterexample and explain why it still may not violate the global theorem.

\]
Equivalently, for \(p=1-q\le1/3\), prove the original lower bound.

\[
P(X)=X(X-p)(X+q),\qquad \mathbb E P(X)=pq(q-p)\sum_i(w_i^2-w_i^3)\ge0,
\]
but continuity near \(0^+\) obstructs a fixed polynomial majorant of \(1_{\{X>0\}}\).
4. Largest-weight induction, which reduces to the pivot/two-tail inequality already recorded.

Please suggest a rigorous route to prove the complement inequality. I am especially interested in whether one can make a threshold-flow/derivative argument from the \(q=2/3\) case, a largest-weight induction with a sharp residual lower-tail lemma, or a finite-dimensional moment/transport argument work.
